caveats: a: O*NET's government-program occupation is broader than veterans' affairs and omits many professional and managerial roles.
a: VA strategy statements identify possible workflows but do not report wage-weighted exposure or verified avoided hours for this NAICS code.
a: The frozen labor-share input is missing because government activity has no comparable receipts denominator.
rho: Strategic plans and pilots are not equivalent to sustained production adoption or avoided hiring.
rho: GAO identifies planning, training, exam-quality, transparency, and misuse-detection weaknesses.
rho: Implementation is likely to differ sharply between routine transactions and high-impact adjudication.
e: The official definition identifies government establishments rather than private firms.
e: Claims vendors, software providers, and veterans-service organizations belong outside this government NAICS population.
e: The frozen LMM firm-count input is also missing.
s5: Leadership turnover and contract recompetes are not control transactions under the screen.
s5: Government reorganizations do not create transferable equity ownership.
s5: A probability conditional on an empty eligible population is undefined.
q: Appropriation changes and budget clawbacks are not customer pricing mechanisms.
q: No eligible commercial operator was identified.
q: Public-service benefits cannot be converted into an owner-retained margin without a commercial revenue model.
d5: Disability claims are only one component of the NAICS service basket.
d5: A single year's PACT-related growth is not a five-year demand forecast.
d5: Claims counts, beneficiary counts, and constant-quality administrative workload are different quantities.
o: The estimate concerns continued operator requirement, not internal labor substitution.
o: Routine federal benefits transactions are more self-serviceable than complex counseling, appeals, and local liaison work.
o: Legal authority and human-review requirements may change during the horizon.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Veterans' affairs offices administer statutory public programs funded by governmental appropriations rather than recurring fees paid by external commercial customers; veterans and dependents are beneficiaries, not customers under an outsourced-service contract.
lens.excluded_activities: Federal, state, and local veterans' affairs agencies and offices, captive government units, hospitals and direct health-care providers, private veterans-service organizations, claims consultants, benefits software vendors, government contractors classified elsewhere, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: No qualifying lower-middle-market outsourced-service firms were identified; the correctly classified US population consists of government establishments administering assistance, training, counseling, and related services for veterans and their families or survivors.

a.bridge: O*NET describes eligibility interviewers across government programs rather than the wage-weighted occupation mix of veterans' affairs administration, while VA's strategy describes agency workflows without measuring task time. I map document intake, classification, extraction, recordkeeping, report preparation, data verification, eligibility support, form assistance, routine inquiries, scheduling, correspondence, and information retrieval to AI exposure, while retaining complex investigation, counseling, appeals, fraud review, discretionary adjudication, supervision, and final legal accountability as less substitutable. A claims-heavy proxy may overstate exposure for counseling and interagency-liaison offices.
caveats: O*NET's government-program occupation is broader than veterans' affairs and omits many professional and managerial roles.
VA strategy statements identify possible workflows but do not report wage-weighted exposure or verified avoided hours for this NAICS code.
The frozen labor-share input is missing because government activity has no comparable receipts denominator.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-linked time-use studies across Veterans Benefits Administration and state or county veterans offices, separating intake, development, rating, appeals, education, loan, rehabilitation, counseling, call-center, liaison, and management work, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The service basket is unusually information- and document-intensive, and VA identifies claims, customer support, and information management as major AI opportunities. Human interviews, sensitive case judgment, explanation of rights, due process, and complex veteran needs bound substitution, and no wage-weighted industry task study was found.

d5.bridge: VA reports 15.6 percent year-over-year growth in disability claims received at an early fiscal-2025 milestone, more than one million claims processed, and 6.9 million veterans and survivors receiving compensation or pension payments; GAO reports more than 6.9 million veterans and family members served by the disability program in fiscal 2025. These measure one program's claims and beneficiaries, not five-year constant-price demand for the full veterans' affairs administration basket. I temper the recent claims surge because PACT Act implementation and timing can produce nonrecurring volume, while aging, case complexity, statutory eligibility, education, rehabilitation, counseling, and survivors' services can sustain workload.
caveats: Disability claims are only one component of the NAICS service basket.
A single year's PACT-related growth is not a five-year demand forecast.
Claims counts, beneficiary counts, and constant-quality administrative workload are different quantities.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year VA and state-office series for unique beneficiaries, initial and supplemental claims, appeals, education enrollments, rehabilitation cases, counseling contacts, processing complexity, and VetPop projections by age and service period would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Near-term claims demand is elevated and program administration remains substantial. The five-year base assumes modest net growth in constant-quality administrative volume rather than compounding the latest surge, with a lower bound allowing normalization and a high bound allowing continuing eligibility expansion and claims intensity.

caveats: The official definition identifies government establishments rather than private firms.
Claims vendors, software providers, and veterans-service organizations belong outside this government NAICS population.
The frozen LMM firm-count input is also missing.
e.change_evidence: A scope change that explicitly admits privatized benefits administrators, or firm-level evidence that commercial contract entities are correctly classified inside NAICS 923140, would be required before e could be defined.
e.rationale: The denominator required by e does not exist for the frozen lens: NAICS 923140 comprises government establishments, while the lens requires transferable LMM firms supplying outsourced services. Assigning zero would turn an undefined share into a measured structural value.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($12,069,228,200) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate concerns continued operator requirement, not internal labor substitution.
Routine federal benefits transactions are more self-serviceable than complex counseling, appeals, and local liaison work.
Legal authority and human-review requirements may change during the horizon.
o.change_evidence: Production evidence showing the share of transactions completed without staff review, self-service adoption and abandonment, appeal and error rates, automated final-decision authority, and closure or consolidation of veterans offices would materially change the value.
o.rationale: AI assistants and straight-through workflows can replace routine inquiries, form handling, document routing, and parts of preliminary eligibility work, and veterans can self-serve many transactions. Statutory program ownership, final high-impact decisions, due process, appeals, fraud controls, counseling, sensitive exceptions, and interagency coordination still require an accountable government operator within five years.

caveats: Appropriation changes and budget clawbacks are not customer pricing mechanisms.
No eligible commercial operator was identified.
Public-service benefits cannot be converted into an owner-retained margin without a commercial revenue model.
q.change_evidence: A revised scope covering a fee-charging outsourced benefits administrator, together with contracts, pricing, renewals, performance guarantees, and realized post-implementation margins, would be required to define q.
q.rationale: Veterans' affairs administrations do not bill external customers under commercial contracts, so there is no defensible operator-retention share after customer pass-through or renewal repricing. Budget savings and improved service capacity are not commercial gross-benefit retention.

rho.bridge: VA reports current OCR and NLP use in disability claims, limited employee access to general-purpose AI, a few production use cases, and plans to redesign major workflows and add production systems; GAO documents both active exploration and long-standing modernization, oversight, transparency, and accountability challenges. I translate this mixed current state into a five-year implementation share of exposed work, discounting strategic ambition for procurement, legacy integration, data quality, appeals, privacy, error detection, governance, training, and change constraints. Agency targets may bias the proxy upward relative to realized deployment.
caveats: Strategic plans and pilots are not equivalent to sustained production adoption or avoided hiring.
GAO identifies planning, training, exam-quality, transparency, and misuse-detection weaknesses.
Implementation is likely to differ sharply between routine transactions and high-impact adjudication.
rho.change_evidence: VA use-case inventory records and Inspector General or GAO evaluations reporting production coverage, utilization, straight-through rates, error and appeal outcomes, review time, hours avoided, user satisfaction, and cost by workflow would materially change implementation.
rho.rationale: Document and inquiry workflows are technically implementable and have executive sponsorship, infrastructure work, and existing automation. High-impact benefit decisions require explainability, quality control, records security, human review, and reliable integration with legacy systems, so implementation should be meaningful but incomplete.

caveats: Leadership turnover and contract recompetes are not control transactions under the screen.
Government reorganizations do not create transferable equity ownership.
A probability conditional on an empty eligible population is undefined.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of correctly classified, privately controlled veterans' affairs administrators in the LMM band and a transaction history for those firms would be required to define s5.
s5.rationale: There is no eligible private firm population for a qualifying control transfer. Elections, appointments, agency reorganizations, appropriations, and contractor changes do not transfer equity control of veterans' affairs administration.

principal_driver: Document-heavy benefits administration and routine veteran inquiries create a large, explicitly targeted AI automation opportunity.
principal_weakness: Government-only classification leaves no transferable LMM firm, commercial contract, or owner-retained benefit under the frozen screen.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as government establishments administering assistance, training, counseling, and other services for veterans and their dependents, heirs, or survivors; supports the lens.
sources.title: NAICS 923140 Administration of Veterans' Affairs

sources.supports: Lists government-benefit eligibility, records, reports, data verification, interviews, rights explanations, forms, fraud review, referrals, and payment monitoring tasks; supports the a proxy.
sources.title: 43-4061.00 - Eligibility Interviewers, Government Programs

sources.supports: Describes current automation, manual workflows, pilots, employee-tool access, claims, customer-support and eligibility use cases, infrastructure, governance, training, and production goals; supports a and rho.
sources.title: Building the Future: VA's Strategy for Adopting High-Impact Artificial Intelligence to Improve Services for Veterans

sources.supports: Documents VA claims-modernization and AI opportunities, oversight and transparency constraints, program scale, and outstanding recommendations; supports rho and d5.
sources.title: VA Disability Benefits: Opportunities and Challenges to Modernizing Technology and Adopting AI

sources.supports: Reports fiscal-2025 claims received and processed, benefit recipients, PACT claims, accuracy, and chatbot availability; supports the d5 proxy and self-service context.
sources.title: VA processes one million disability claims faster than ever before

title: Administration of Veterans' Affairs
